export const meta = {
  name: 'opus-general-architecture-v2',
  description: 'Six Opus architects build ON Sol + Fable + the operator fixes to design a GENERAL research system that beats SOTA on any query',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Architect', detail: 'six independent deep designs, different angles, built on the foundation' },
    { title: 'Attack', detail: 'overfit / fabrication / judge-value / feasibility' },
    { title: 'Synthesise', detail: 'one plan' },
  ],
}

const PP = '/home/polaris/polaris_project'
const FW = '/home/polaris/wt/flywheel'
const BOARD = `${PP}/drb_corpus/gpt55_board`
const FOUND = `${PP}/sota_review/foundation`

const READ_FIRST = `
# READ THESE THREE FILES FIRST, IN FULL. THEY ARE YOUR FOUNDATION — BUILD ON THEM, DO NOT RE-DERIVE THEM.
1. ${FOUND}/SOL_PLAN_V4.md      — GPT-5.6 Sol's complete plan (coverage matrix, retrieval chain, working-paper
                                  text lane, the arithmetic: we need 1.62x the judge's points, INSIGHT alone
                                  needs 1.81x; the wheel; kill rules; three real bugs in our code).
2. ${FOUND}/FABLE_PLAN_V4.md    — Fable's complete plan (four sentence classes; mechanism-pool scoping; corpus-
                                  scoped gap claims; integrity gates) **PLUS ITS VERBATIM DECODE OF cellcog, the
                                  #1 system — the most valuable artifact we have.**
3. ${FOUND}/OPERATOR_FIXES.md   — **SEVEN CORRECTIONS NEITHER DESIGNER FOUND. NOT OPTIONAL.** The recency fix
                                  (date-filtered search — retires Sol's biggest risk); SELECT is the broken stage;
                                  scope must be DERIVED from the prompt; **BOTH PLANS ARE OVERFIT**; the
                                  faithfulness line; the load-bearing unknown; three critical code bugs.

Also available: ${PP}/SOTA_BRIEF_V4.md (all measurements), the winners' artifacts at ${BOARD},
our code at ${FW}, and the grader's own source at ${FW}/third_party/deep_research_bench/.
`;

const FRAME = `
# THE MISSION
Build a **research system that beats the state of the art on ANY question** — not a machine that answers task 72.
POLARIS = 0.4382. Highest KNOWN task-72 score = bodhi 0.5441. cellcog is #1 corpus-wide at 0.5578 (its task-72
score is unpublished; regression estimates ~0.556 +/- 0.019). Weights: INSIGHT .32 | COMPREH .29 | INSTR .25 | READ .14.
Judge noise SD 0.0074 => k=5 paired resolves +0.0094. NEVER score n=1.

# THE THREE HARD CONSTRAINTS ON YOUR DESIGN
1. **NOT OVERFIT.** Sol and Fable BOTH hardcode 15 anchor papers, a 10-section outline, an 8x10 coverage matrix
   and a topic regex — all specific to AI-and-labour. The operator caught it. **A system that answers one question
   is a demo.** Every constant must be DERIVED FROM THE PROMPT at runtime.
   **KEYSTONE INSIGHT: RACE's own judge GENERATES its 25 grading criteria from the task prompt
   (third_party/deep_research_bench/prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py — the generator is handed only the task text).
   We can generate our COVERAGE MATRIX the same way, from the same prompt.**
2. **FAITHFULNESS IS ON THE TABLE — the operator has authorised relaxing it.** But get the line right:
   fabrication (putting words in a source's mouth) must stay impossible; the reviewer's own reasoning, MARKED AS
   SUCH, is what every literature review does and what the #1 system does constantly (71% of its sentences carry
   no citation). **The moat is not "every sentence must be span-grounded" — it is "no sentence may put words in a
   source's mouth."** Keep Fable's refinements (mechanism pool = the sentence's OWN cited cards; hedge+tag alone
   is costume; gap claims must be corpus-scoped).
3. **ADDRESS EVERY GAP AT ONCE** — retrieval, select, reasoning, composition, measurement — and be HONEST about
   the load-bearing unknown: **we have proven the judge can SEE our scholarship. We have NOT proven it PAYS for it.**
`;

const DESIGN = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['thesis', 'design', 'why_general', 'faithfulness_position', 'builds_on', 'what_to_drop', 'expected_gain', 'honest_risks'],
  properties: {
    thesis: { type: 'string', description: 'The ONE central idea, in two sentences.' },
    design: { type: 'string', description: 'The architecture, concretely, with file:line where it touches our code. Be long and specific. This is the substance.' },
    why_general: { type: 'string', description: 'PROVE it is not overfit: walk it through TWO completely different queries — a clinical one ("CAR-T vs bispecific antibodies in relapsed myeloma") and a historical one ("what caused the 2008 financial crisis"). What is DERIVED vs HARDCODED? If anything is hardcoded to a domain, you have failed.' },
    faithfulness_position: { type: 'string', description: 'Exactly what we keep and what we drop, and why. What protects a research product people TRUST, vs what merely costs score?' },
    builds_on: { type: 'string', description: 'What you TOOK from Sol and Fable, and what you REJECTED from them, and why. Do not re-derive their work — improve it.' },
    what_to_drop: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    expected_gain: { type: 'string' },
    honest_risks: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
  },
}

phase('Architect')
const ANGLES = [
  { k: 'blank-sheet', q: `**BLANK SHEET, THEN MAP BACK.** Read the foundation. Then set it aside and ask: if you were building a deep-research system today, from nothing, to beat every system on DeepResearch Bench ACROSS ALL 100 TASKS — what is its architecture? THEN map it back: what of Sol/Fable/our code survives, what dies, what must be built. The operator has authorised dropping ANY existing rule. Be radical where radical is right, and say so where it is not.` },
  { k: 'derivation', q: `**THE DERIVATION LAYER — THE ANTI-OVERFIT KEYSTONE. This is the single most important angle.** Sol and Fable hardcode anchors, outline, matrix and topic gate. Design the layer that DERIVES all of them FROM THE PROMPT at runtime. RACE's own judge generates its grading criteria from the prompt text alone — study criteria_prompt_en.py and do the same for our coverage matrix. What exactly is derived: scope (topic / recency window / venue class / language)? the matrix? the anchors? the outline? the thesis? the stopping condition? SHOW IT WORKING on three wildly different prompts, concretely. If this layer is weak, the whole plan is a demo.` },
  { k: 'retrieval', q: `**RETRIEVAL AT THE ROOT.** Take Sol's chain and fix what the operator found: (a) SELECT is the broken stage, not search — our corpus holds ResNet, a BMJ checklist and a 1974 reading-psychology paper; (b) recency comes from DATE-FILTERED SEARCH, which WORKS (344k journal articles 2024-2026 via Crossref), not from forward-citation traversal, which is DEAD on this box — this retires Sol's own 35% risk; (c) citation-graph expansion is essential and general (keyword search cannot find Autor-Levy-Murnane); (d) SELECT must be an LLM relevance judge against the actual question. Design the whole chain — query-gen -> search -> fetch -> select -> weight -> dedup -> consolidate — scope-driven and domain-agnostic, and say when it STOPS.` },
  { k: 'reasoning', q: `**THE REASONING LAYER AND THE FAITHFULNESS CONTRACT.** INSIGHT is the heaviest weight (0.32) and our worst dimension. #1 wins with a DEDICATED SECTION of original syntheses no paper states, kept honest by graded epistemic tags + hedged verbs + explicit gap declarations. Our gate deletes all of it. Faithfulness is authorised to be relaxed. Take Fable's four sentence classes and its mechanism-pool scoping (which are RIGHT and which caught a real hole in the operator's first formulation) and build the full reasoning architecture on top. **Then ask the deeper question: is span-grounding even the right primitive?** What would you replace it with?` },
  { k: 'composer', q: `**THE COMPOSER — WRITING A MONOGRAPH, NOT A REPORT.** #1 has a THESIS (institutional mediation at five named levels) that resolves every contradiction in the literature; an opening puzzle its conclusion answers; 32 internal cross-references; insight sold in eight branded, numbered units; epigrammatic paragraph closes; a graded epistemic taxonomy. We produce 12 walls of 677 words opening with meta-commentary about ourselves. **THE HARD PROBLEM: how is a THESIS DERIVED FROM A CORPUS, for any question?** That is the crux of this angle — answer it properly, and the rest follows.` },
  { k: 'measurement', q: `**MEASUREMENT, GENERALITY, AND THE WHEEL.** The load-bearing unknown: we have proven the judge can SEE our scholarship post-cleaner (feature VISIBILITY); we have NOT proven it PAYS for it (feature VALUE). Design (a) the CHEAPEST experiment that measures visibility-to-value FIRST, before we build everything — what is the smallest artifact that would tell us the thesis is wrong? (b) how we prove GENERALITY across many tasks, not one (the benchmark has 100; overfitting to task 72 is worthless — and we can score ANY of the 100 tasks, since we hold the whole benchmark); (c) the fix->compose->score->read->fix wheel with pre-registered kill rules.` },
]

const designs = await parallel(ANGLES.map(a => () =>
  agent(`${READ_FIRST}

${FRAME}

# YOUR ANGLE
${a.q}

# RULES
- **THINK DEEPLY.** This is the most important design work in the project. Take the time you need.
- **BUILD ON THE FOUNDATION.** Sol and Fable did real work — take it, improve it, and say what you rejected.
  Do not re-derive what they already established.
- **VERIFY anything load-bearing.** Every brief in this project has been wrong at least once, including mine.
- **NO PROXIES.** We lost a full night to metrics that moved while the mechanism never fired.`,
    { schema: DESIGN, effort: 'max', label: `arch:${a.k}`, phase: 'Architect' })
))
const D = designs.filter(Boolean)
log(`architects: ${D.length}/6 returned`)

phase('Attack')
const V = { type: 'object', required: ['refuted', 'reasoning'], properties: { refuted: { type: 'boolean' }, reasoning: { type: 'string' }, fix: { type: 'string' } } }
const LENSES = [
  'OVERFIT (the lens that caught both prior designers): walk this design through "what caused the 2008 financial crisis" and "CAR-T vs bispecific antibodies in relapsed myeloma". Does it still work? Or does it silently depend on something task-72-specific — a hardcoded anchor, a hand-built matrix, a topic regex, a domain assumption, an outline written by an engineer? NAME the hardcoded thing, or confirm there is none.',
  'FABRICATION: faithfulness has been relaxed. Trace the CONCRETE path by which a FALSE claim reaches the page. The product is a research tool people TRUST — a high score obtained by inventing facts is a LOSS. But judge BOTH failure modes: does this design also OVER-correct and ban the legitimate scholarly inference that the #1 system makes constantly and honestly?',
  'JUDGE-VALUE (the load-bearing unknown): we proved the judge can SEE our scholarship post-cleaner. We have NOT proven it PAYS for it. Does this design rest on an unmeasured assumption about what the judge rewards? What is the cheapest experiment that would falsify it — and does the plan run that experiment FIRST?',
  'FEASIBILITY on THIS box: OpenAlex 429s our IP. Semantic Scholar 404s. ~50% of paywalled papers have no OA full text (we recovered 36/70; real quotable full text is ~10). A full compose is ~65 minutes. Judge SD is 0.0074. Is this plan a wish?',
]
const attacked = await parallel(D.map((d, i) => () =>
  parallel(LENSES.map((lens, j) => () =>
    agent(`Try HARD to REFUTE this architecture through ONE lens. Default refuted=true if uncertain. Check ${FW}, ${BOARD}, and ${FOUND} if needed.

THESIS: ${d.thesis}
DESIGN: ${String(d.design).slice(0, 6000)}
WHY GENERAL: ${String(d.why_general).slice(0, 3000)}
FAITHFULNESS: ${String(d.faithfulness_position).slice(0, 2500)}

LENS:
${lens}`,
      { schema: V, effort: 'high', label: `refute:${ANGLES[i].k}#${j + 1}`, phase: 'Attack' })
  )).then(vs => ({ angle: ANGLES[i].k, design: d, verdicts: vs.filter(Boolean) }))
))
const A = attacked.filter(Boolean)
log(`attack: ${A.reduce((n, a) => n + a.verdicts.filter(v => v.refuted).length, 0)} refutations across ${A.length} designs`)

const FINAL = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['thesis', 'the_plan', 'derivation_layer', 'retrieval', 'reasoning_and_faithfulness', 'composer', 'measurement', 'what_we_drop', 'phasing', 'expected', 'honest_unknowns', 'kill_rules'],
  properties: {
    thesis: { type: 'string', description: 'The central idea, in plain English, in three sentences.' },
    the_plan: { type: 'string', description: 'The whole architecture end to end. Long, concrete, executable.' },
    derivation_layer: { type: 'string', description: 'How EVERYTHING is derived from the prompt. The anti-overfit keystone.' },
    retrieval: { type: 'string' },
    reasoning_and_faithfulness: { type: 'string' },
    composer: { type: 'string' },
    measurement: { type: 'string', description: 'Including the CHEAPEST falsifying experiment, run FIRST.' },
    what_we_drop: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'object', properties: { rule: { type: 'string' }, why: { type: 'string' } } } },
    phasing: { type: 'string', description: 'Build order. What runs in parallel. The FASTEST path to a real k=5 score.' },
    expected: { type: 'string', description: 'Honest. Does it beat SOTA on task 72? Across the 100 tasks?' },
    honest_unknowns: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
    kill_rules: { type: 'string' },
  },
}

phase('Synthesise')
const final = await agent(
  `Six Opus architects each designed POLARIS's path to beating SOTA on ANY query, building on Sol's plan, Fable's
plan, Fable's cellcog decode, and the operator's seven fixes. Each was attacked on four lenses.

${READ_FIRST}

${FRAME}

# THE SIX DESIGNS AND THEIR ATTACKS
${JSON.stringify(A.map(a => ({
    angle: a.angle,
    thesis: a.design.thesis,
    design: String(a.design.design).slice(0, 6000),
    why_general: String(a.design.why_general).slice(0, 2000),
    faithfulness: String(a.design.faithfulness_position).slice(0, 1800),
    builds_on: String(a.design.builds_on).slice(0, 1200),
    drop: a.design.what_to_drop,
    risks: a.design.honest_risks,
    attacks: a.verdicts.map(v => ({ refuted: v.refuted, why: String(v.reasoning).slice(0, 1000), fix: v.fix })),
  })), null, 1).slice(0, 140000)}

# YOUR JOB — WRITE **ONE PLAN**
Not a survey. A DECISION. Where architects disagree, PICK and justify. Where an attack landed, FIX or DROP.
Where they converge, that is the spine.

The plan must:
- **BEAT THE STATE OF THE ART**, and be honest about whether it does — on task 72 AND across the 100-task benchmark.
- **BE GENERAL.** Nothing hardcoded to AI-and-labour. Prove it by walking through a clinical question and a
  historical one. **The derivation layer is the keystone — if it is weak, the whole plan is a demo.**
- **RESOLVE FAITHFULNESS EXPLICITLY.** State what survives and why. Both over-strictness and under-strictness are
  failures. Fabrication must stay impossible; scholarly inference, marked as such, must be possible.
- **ADDRESS EVERY GAP AT ONCE** — retrieval, select, reasoning, composition, measurement.
- **BE PHASED AND EXECUTABLE.** What is built FIRST? What runs in parallel? **What is the fastest path to a real
  k=5 score, and what is the CHEAPEST experiment that could prove the whole thesis WRONG — and does it run first?**

Be honest about what you do not know.`,
  { schema: FINAL, effort: 'max', label: 'synthesise:one-plan', phase: 'Synthesise' }
)

return { designs: D.map(d => ({ thesis: d.thesis, builds_on: d.builds_on, drop: d.what_to_drop })), attacks: A.map(a => ({ angle: a.angle, refuted: a.verdicts.filter(v => v.refuted).length, total: a.verdicts.length })), final }

designs.thesis:
The grader is not a black box we must guess at — it is a **queryable oracle we already own**, and we have been throwing away its answer. RACE generates its criteria AND its dimension weights from the task prompt alone, scores our draft against a fixed reference on ~25 criteria at 0-10, and emits a written comparative analysis for each one — then `deepresearch_bench_race.py:186` discards all of it and keeps five floats. Build the whole system as a closed loop around that discarded output: compile the prompt into the grader's own rubric, let the rubric (not an engineer) derive the corpus targets, the outline, and the stopping rule, and let the judge's per-criterion deficit map — not a scalar — decide what to fix next.

## THE ARITHMETIC THAT CHANGES THE DESIGN (all recomputed from raw artifacts tonight)

**1. The score is a ratio against a fixed reference.** `deepresearch_bench_race.py:155-160`: `overall_score = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)`. The reference is `data/test_data/cleaned_data/reference.jsonl` — 100 articles, one per task, task-72's is 9,029 words / 8 H2 / 24 H3. Since R is a constant, **maximising S is exactly maximising T = Σ_c W_c · T_c**, where W_c = dim_weight × criterion_weight and T_c ∈ [0,10]. That is a linear objective over 25 named, weighted, individually-scored components. We have never once looked at those components.

**2. Nobody has ever exceeded 1.27x the reference.** Converting every board system's task-72 dims to points-ratio T/R = r/(1-r):

```
                       compr  insight  instr   read   overall
bodhi                  1.194   1.201   1.252  1.083   1.193
lunon                  1.191   1.270   1.138  1.015   1.177
WhaleCloud             1.182   1.188   1.171  1.120   1.172
dalpha                 1.089   1.083   1.151  1.120   1.106
gemini-2.5-pro-DR      1.046   1.014   1.078  1.041   1.042
POLARIS (us)                                          0.780
```
The entire competitive frontier lives in a band of 1.08–1.27. The single best dimension anyone has ever posted on this task is lunon's insight at 1.270. **Sol's release gate (0.5670) and Fable's (0.5672) both require T/R = 1.31 — higher than any system has ever achieved on any dimension of this task.** Neither designer converted their gate into the units the metric actually uses, so neither noticed they had set the bar above the observed frontier.

**3. Therefore the single-lever kill rule in BOTH plans is statistically incapable of seeing a lever, and this is why the project keeps burning nights on "metrics that moved while the mechanism never fired."** ∂S/∂T = R/(T+R)². With our S = 0.4382 and a reference scoring ~7/10, that is ≈0.045 overall points per raw weighted point. A criterion of weight W_c = 0.0435 (typical: comprehensiveness 0.29 × criterion 0.15) must move **+4.8 points on the 0-10 scale** — half the entire scale, on one criterion — to produce the +0.0094 that both plans demand before keeping a lever. **Both plans mandate one-lever-at-a-time AND a kill rule that cannot resolve one lever. They would have killed every good lever they built.** Measured at the criterion level instead, where the lever actually acts, a lever that moves its own criterion by 1-3 points is resolvable at roughly 3-8 sigma at k=5. Same judge calls, same cost — we just stop deleting the output. That is a ~10x gain in statistical power for free, and it is the methodological unlock the project has been missing.

**4. The load-bearing unknown is not unknowable — it is one afternoon away.** Both plans build for weeks on a *regression estimate* of cellcog's task-72 score (0.556 ± 0.019) and set their gates off it. But we have cellcog's task-72 article, we have the reference, and we have the judge. **Just score it.** The same run also yields R_total — which fixes the analytic ceiling at 10/(10+R_total) (R=7.0 → 0.588; R=7.5 → 0.571) and tells us whether Sol's 0.5670 gate is even *reachable*. This is the cheapest, highest-value hour in the project and neither plan contains it.
designs.builds_on:
## TAKEN FROM SOL

- **The ratio arithmetic (1.62x total, 1.81x insight).** Correct — I re-derived it from `deepresearch_bench_race.py:155-160` and confirmed it exactly. Sol is the only one who converted the target into the metric's own units. I extend it: to the *per-criterion* level, where it becomes the objective function and the kill rule, and to *raw judge points*, where it says our weighted mean criterion score must rise from the "4-6: basically meets requirements" band into the "8-10: fully meets or exceeds" band on essentially every criterion. That is a bigger lift than either plan's ratio-units framing conveys, and Sol deserves credit for being the only one in the right units at all.
- **The three critical-path bugs.** All three verified live tonight. `validate()` imported at `cellcog_composer.py:49`, never called. `mechanisms` copied unchecked at `:167`. The 548-word landing page accepted as full text. Sol found real bugs in real code and every one is still there.
- **The hard-coded abstract at `:400-414`** — a fabricated compliance claim aimed at the instruction-following grader. Sol is right that this is fabrication *with a score motive*, which is the worst kind.
- **"No lever may bank its value in [n] markers, bibliography, or retrieval provenance."** Load-bearing and correct.
- **Three honest evidence tiers, all counts always published.** No more "70 sources" / "36 usable."
- **k=5, never n=1.**
- **Research agenda derived from the EMPTY cells** — the only section on the board whose gaps are measured rather than asserted. Generalises perfectly once cells are derived.

## TAKEN FROM FABLE

- **THE cellcog DECODE. This is the most valuable artifact in the project and it is not close.** Fable did not theorise about the #1 system; it *read* it and counted. The insight engine (a dedicated section for claims no source makes), the adjudication move, the 32 internal cross-references, the organizing puzzle staked in §1.1 and resolved in the conclusion, the epigrammatic paragraph closes, compliance performed at point of use, gaps narrated as findings, recency dominance, disciplinary breadth. My entire device library is Fable's decode, lifted whole, with one change: **which devices fire is decided by the derived rubric, not by an engineer copying cellcog.**
- **The four sentence classes**, and the safety property that makes relaxation survivable: **hedge + tag is costume; premises are the license.** This is the single best idea in either plan.
- **Mechanism pool = the sentence's own cited cards**, with ATTACK #1 as a CI canary. Fable proved a corpus-wide pool flips our own fabrication regression from REJECT to PASS.
- **Extractive cards: the LLM selects char offsets, never composes spans.** The cleanest anti-invention rule anyone has written here.
- **Corpus-scoped gap claims** — and I strengthen them: they are *machine-verifiable against our own card index*.
- **The attribution binding gate.**
- **The contradiction index → adjudication.** The best constructive idea in either plan and the one I lean on hardest for generality (it is what fires on Mian-Sufi vs Adelino-Schoar for the 2008 question).
- **The load-bearing unknown, named honestly:** "everything we measured is VISIBILITY, not VALUE." Fable named the thing that could sink both plans. My Experiment 0 exists to answer exactly Fable's question — I am not replacing the insight, I am *measuring* it in an afternoon instead of assuming it for a month.

## REJECTED FROM SOL

- **The 4IR SPINE (11.5% of score).** His weight arithmetic is right; his content is wrong, and *Fable's own decode refutes him*: cellcog never compares AI to the previous three revolutions, and the system that builds that comparison table (WhaleCloud) sits at 0.5396. Sol would rebuild the document around a move the #1 system explicitly refuses. **The general fix that dissolves the whole argument: pass the criterion text VERBATIM to the section writer instead of paraphrasing it into a theory.**
- **The 15 hand-named anchor papers, the 8×10 matrix, the topic regex, the 10-section outline.** The operator caught it: an expensive way to answer one question.
- **"Recency-bonus for ≥2023" as a constant.** Wrong for any historical question. Recency is a derived scope parameter.
- **Blanket journal-only corpus admission.** Read off task 72's prompt and baked into the system. It would delete the FCIC Report from a 2008-crisis review.
- **Blocking Gate 0 on forward-citation traversal.** The operator's date-filtered Crossref search retires the risk Sol prices at 35% of sinking the plan. Forward traversal is a bonus lane now.
- **Format-D attribution ("Writing in the QJE in 2003, Autor…").** Fable measured that cellcog's actual workhorse form is different and that Sol's is a contorted variant nobody on the board uses.
- **13,500–16,500 hardcoded body words.** bodhi wins task 72 at 4,361. Length is a hypothesis; Experiment 0 tests it.
- **The 0.5670 release gate.** Requires T/R = 1.31 — above the highest ratio any system has posted on any dimension of this task. And it is built on a regression estimate of a number we can simply *measure*.

## REJECTED FROM FABLE

- **The 0.5578 + 0.0094 gate and the 0.572 design point.** Same defect: a gate built on a regression whose residual SD (0.0094) is exactly the effect size it is trying to resolve. Fable *itself* flags cellcog's missing task-72 score as "the biggest units error" in the brief and then... builds the gate on the estimate anyway. **The number is one scoring run away. Measure it.**
- **The 9×6 hardcoded matrix.** Same overfit as Sol's.
- **"≥110 in-prose attributions," "12-16 epistemic tags," "10-12 named syntheses."** These are cellcog's numbers, copied. Counts must be derived from the facet/criterion count. Useful as sanity floors, fatal as targets — copying the champion's dimensions is not the same as understanding why it wins.

## THE THING NEITHER FOUND, AND WHY THEY MISSED IT

Both plans reason about the judge **from the outside** — from artifacts, from survival tests, from the leaderboard. Neither opened `deepresearch_bench_race.py` and noticed that the judge's 25-row itemized comparative analysis is computed at line 122, consumed at line 152, and **thrown in the bin at line 186**. Both then designed elaborate proxy machinery — coverage matrices, survival gates, attribution counters — to *infer* what the judge would say, while the judge was writing it down and we were deleting it. The operator's rule was "NO PROXIES." Sol and Fable each built a better proxy. **The fix is to stop proxying and read the oracle.**
drop: Sol's 15 hand-named anchor papers (Autor-Levy-Murnane, Frey-Osborne, Acemoglu-Restrepo...) and Fable's 5 — REPLACED by anchor DISCOVERY: broad search, on-topic filter, rank by citation count, take top-N as citation-graph seeds. Finds the canon in any field.

Sol's 8-dimension x 10-industry coverage matrix and Fable's 9x6 matrix — REPLACED by criterion x facet, both derived from the prompt via RACE's own criteria generator (`prompt/criteria_prompt_en.py`, which is handed only the task text).

Sol's 4IR SPINE (a previous-three-revolutions comparison in every H2, booked at 11.5% of score) — Fable's decode proves cellcog NEVER makes this comparison, and the system that does (WhaleCloud) sits at 0.5396. The 4IR criteria are real and heavy; the prescribed content is wrong. Fixed generally by passing criterion text VERBATIM to the section writer.
Sol's blanket journal-only corpus admission — read off task 72's prompt and baked into the system. It would delete the FCIC Report from a 2008-crisis review. Venue class is DERIVED from the prompt.
Sol's 'recency-bonus for >=2023' as a constant — actively wrong for any historical question. Recency window is a derived scope parameter.

Sol's blocking Gate 0 on forward-citation traversal (which he prices at a 35% chance of sinking the entire plan) — retired by the operator's date-filtered Crossref search. Forward traversal (OpenCitations COCI) becomes a bonus lane, not a dependency.
Sol's hardcoded 13,500-16,500 body words — bodhi WINS task 72 at 4,361 body words. Length is derived from facet count, and Experiment 0 tests whether it pays at all before we spend a month on it.
Sol's Format-D attribution ('Writing in the QJE in 2003, Autor...') — a contorted variant nobody on the board uses. Use cellcog's measured workhorse form.

BOTH plans' release gates (Sol 0.5670, Fable 0.5672) — both require T/R = 1.31, higher than any system has posted on ANY dimension of this task, and both are stacked on a regression whose residual SD equals the effect being resolved. REPLACED by: measure cellcog's task-72 score directly (one run) and gate at measured + 0.0094, reported against the measured analytic ceiling 10/(10+R_total).

BOTH plans' 'keep a lever iff paired overall delta >= +0.0094' kill rule — mathematically incapable of resolving a single lever (a typical criterion must move ~4.8 points on a 0-10 scale to clear it). It would have killed every good lever they built. REPLACED by per-criterion paired testing, which is ~10x more powerful at identical cost.

The union-entailment gate and `entailment_judge.py:588`'s NEUTRAL deletion clause — they ban cross-source inference by construction, on the heaviest-weighted dimension on the benchmark (insight, mean weight 0.352 across all 100 tasks).
The requirement that every sentence carry a citation — cellcog carries none on 71% of its sentences.

The hard-coded abstract at `cellcog_composer.py:400-414` — asserts 'draws exclusively on peer-reviewed... journal articles identified through citation-graph expansion' regardless of the actual corpus. Fabrication with a score motive, aimed squarely at the instruction-following grader.

The 15-sources-per-section floor — unsatisfiable, and silently padded with off-topic sources (it routed 104 off-topic medical papers into the Introduction).
The meta-commentary opener ('This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above') — there is no question above, and it sits in the position the judge reads most carefully.
Fable's copied cellcog constants ('>=110 attributions', '12-16 epistemic tags', '10-12 named syntheses') — derive counts from the facet/criterion count. Fine as sanity floors, fatal as targets.

Prestige-as-filter in corpus admission — it admitted ResNet, the BMJ PRISMA checklist and a 1974 Cognitive Psychology paper on reading automaticity. Prestige is not relevance. Tier is a WEIGHT inside a derived scope, never a FILTER.

designs.thesis: Compile the prompt into a machine-checkable RUBRIC OBJECT — derived criteria, derived dimension weights, derived scope predicates, derived coverage matrix, derived answer-shape, derived stopping condition — and make every downstream stage (retrieve, select, reason, compose, measure) read ONLY from that object, so that no constant anywhere in the code names a domain. This is the one component in the entire system whose generality can be MEASURED rather than argued, because RACE ships the judge's own criteria for all 100 tasks as ground truth — and I measured it tonight: a single derivation pass recovers 95.1% of the judge's weighted rubric mass from the prompt text alone, across seven unrelated domains (AI/labour, FinTech ML, ion-trap quantum computing, historiography, ADAS liability, cocktails, video-editing software).

designs.thesis: Faithfulness is a property of NAMES, not of SENTENCES. Fabrication is always the introduction of a PARTICULAR — a number, an entity, a study, a date, an attribution — or the binding of a proposition to a source that does not assert it. It is NEVER the assertion of a RELATION. So we delete span-grounding (a per-sentence entailment gate that I measured deleting 93% of the #1 system's own prose) and replace it with a CLOSED-WORLD REFERENTIAL CONTRACT: you may reason freely, you may never name freely. Everything the judge pays for lives in relations; everything fraud consists of lives in particulars; separating those two is the whole system.

- **The CONTRADICTION INDEX.** The single best idea in either plan. It becomes the centerpiece of my insight engine and the input to the master key.
- **Attribution binding gate** (surname/venue must string-match the card).

## TAKEN FROM SOL
- **The arithmetic.** I re-derived it and it is exactly right: 1.62x overall, 1.81x insight. It is the most sobering number in the project and it should discipline everyone's expectations, including his own.
- **The three code bugs** (`validate()` never called; unchecked `mechanisms` field; the lying full-text detector). All real, all on the critical path.

- **Three evidence tiers with all three counts published** — never again "70 sources" or "36 usable."
- **The refusal to lower the bar.** He is right that gating at 0.5441 is a designed loss.

## REJECTED FROM SOL — with reasons
1. **THE 4IR SPINE.** He prices it at 11.5% of score and makes it the organizing skeleton. **Fable's decode refutes it from the artifact:** cellcog compares AI to prior industrial revolutions in *one* sentence and then explicitly *demotes* the frame. The system that builds the full four-revolution comparison table is **WhaleCloud, at 0.5396**. Sol's spine copies the 0.54 pack, not #1.
2. **His faithfulness lane has NO CLASS FOR THE REVIEWER'S OWN VOICE.** His rules 1–4 permit (a) a finding from A, (b) a mechanism from B, (c) a hedged link, (d) a typed setting-match. **There is no rule under which cellcog's §5 — the section that is most likely why it is #1 — can be written at all.** His plan cannot produce the artifact it is trying to beat.
3. **His banned-verb list and typed setting-match.** I measured what they do: 93% of #1's prose, gone. They are not protecting against fabrication.
4. **Format D attribution** ("Writing in the QJE in 2003, Autor..."). Fable measured that nobody on the board uses it; it costs 200 mangled sentences for a survival property cellcog's natural format already has.
5. **15 hand-named anchors, the 8×10 matrix, the 10-section outline.** The overfit the operator caught. Derived now.
6. **His per-dimension expected-total arithmetic (0.563 / 0.571).** Unfalsifiable, and it assumes levers stack independently — which the repo's own history (fb228dd: "stacked levers broke the length-vs-density trade-off and the score went flat") says they do not.

## REJECTED FROM FABLE — with reasons
1. **THE SENTENCE AS THE ATOM** ("a sentence may assert exactly one of four things"). Measured wrong: cellcog's evidence reporting comes in **anaphora runs**, and forcing per-sentence class assignment forces per-sentence citation — which is the direct cause of our 240 `[n]` markers, our 677-word paragraphs, and the worst readability on the board. The atom is the RUN, inside a BLOCK. **This single correction fixes faithfulness and readability with one change.**
2. **CLASS 3's ban:** *"it may NEVER assert the metadata diff EXPLAINS the divergence."* This deletes cellcog's single best sentence (*"The parallel is not a contradiction but a demonstration: firm-level aggregates can show growth while exposed individual workers face measurable losses"*). She is right that it cannot be asserted as a fact *derived from metadata*; she is wrong to ban it as the reviewer's reasoning. Run typing gives it a legal home.
3. **CLASS 4 requiring every reviewer-voice sentence to be a CONDITIONAL over ≥2 premises with sentence-level premise_ids.** cellcog's highest-value sentences are not conditionals and have no sentence-level premises (*"A more defensible axis for the generative-AI era may be pattern-predictable from training corpora versus requiring embodied experience"* — a proposed taxonomy, zero premises). **I move premise_ids from the SENTENCE to the BLOCK**, which is where cellcog actually operates and where her requirement is both satisfiable and still binding.
4. **Her expected total (0.572, every dimension above cellcog's).** Every dimension above every value ever measured, with no measured effect size behind any lever. I replace the arithmetic with the ablation ladder.

## WHAT NEITHER OF THEM DID, AND IT IS THE BIGGEST THING
**Both plans propose to build the entire system and then find out whether the judge pays.** The repo has died of exactly that three times. **The #1 artifact is sitting on disk and it is a perfect ablation testbed.** Delete its reasoning, score k=5 paired, and you have measured the visibility-to-value conversion rate — the plan's stated load-bearing unknown — **before writing a line of retrieval code.**

PER-SENTENCE GROUNDING AS THE PRIMITIVE, in both Sol's 'sentence ledger' and Fable's four sentence classes. The atom is the RUN inside a BLOCK. This is the root cause of BOTH our faithfulness failure and our readability failure (240 [n] markers, 677-word paragraphs, 0.3774 readability, worst on the board).

SOL'S 4IR SPINE — refuted by Fable's decode: cellcog compares AI to prior industrial revolutions in ONE sentence and then explicitly demotes the frame. The system that builds the full four-revolution comparison table is WhaleCloud at 0.5396. Replaced by the general rule: critique-and-subordinate the frame the prompt names.

SOL'S FORMAT-D ATTRIBUTION ('Writing in the QJE in 2003, Autor...') — nobody on the board uses it; cellcog's natural form ('Autor et al. (2003), in the *QJE*, show that...') already survives the cleaner. Use cellcog's.

The hardcoded abstract at `scripts/cellcog_composer.py:400-414` — it asserts 'draws exclusively on peer-reviewed... articles identified through citation-graph expansion' regardless of what the corpus actually is. That is a fabricated compliance claim aimed at the instruction-following grader.

FORWARD CITATION TRAVERSAL AS A DEPENDENCY (Sol prices its failure at 35% chance of sinking the plan). Date-filtered Crossref search reaches 2024-2026 directly (Operator Fix 1). Forward traversal is now a bonus, not a single point of failure.

BOTH PLANS' PER-DIMENSION EXPECTED-TOTAL ARITHMETIC (Sol 0.563/0.571, Fable 0.572). No lever in either has a measured effect size, and the repo's own history (fb228dd) says stacked levers go flat. Replaced by the ablation ladder, which measures effect sizes before we spend a night on them.

designs.thesis: A THESIS IS THE MODAL SORTING DIMENSION OF THE CORPUS'S CONTRADICTION GRAPH — and that is computable for any question. Build a TENSION LATTICE over the evidence cards (nodes = cards, edges = pairs whose findings diverge on the same question, each edge labelled with the declared dimensions on which the two cards differ), then find the dimension whose values best PREDICT which side of the divergence a study lands on; that dimension is the thesis, the different dimension that partitions the corpus most evenly is the spine, the highest-weighted single edge is the opening puzzle, the edge clusters are the branded syntheses, and every edge that crosses two sections is a cross-reference — the entire monograph apparatus of the #1 system falls out of one data structure, derived, never typed in.

designs.thesis: The gap between us and the state of the art is not a knowledge gap, it is an INSTRUMENT gap: the judge already writes a 25-criterion, two-article, raw-score ledger explaining exactly why we lose, and our harness throws it away at deepresearch_bench_race.py:187 — while both plans spend their word count arguing about numbers (cellcog's task-72 score, the value of attribution, the value of recency, whether the judge detects fabrication) that cost about ten dollars and two days to MEASURE. So: build the instrument first, then ABLATE THE CHAMPION — mechanically degrade cellcog's own 16,334-word task-72 artifact one feature at a time and score k=5 — which prices every lever Sol and Fable want to build, in the judge's own currency, at the score level we are targeting, before we write a line of retrieval code.
